text: In this video, we will be describing the daycare laparoscopic myomectomy on a patient who presented with a large posterior wall uterine fibroid.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic myomectomy by mobilizing the sigmoid colon, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues to access the surgical site.
The sigmoid colon is fully mobilized away from the pelvis, allowing the assistant to actively participate in the pelvic surgery without obstruction.
This is a routine step which we follow for all the pelvic procedures at our clinic.
The surgeon opens the space between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to access the uterine artery at its origin.
The uterine artery is identified in the pararectal space, visually distinguished as the only structure crossing from lateral to medial.
The surgeon separates the uterine artery from the underlying ureter and dissects the surrounding fascia to isolate the artery.
The surgeon isolates the uterine artery, preparing to either clip or coagulate the vessel as part of the myomectomy procedure.
The ureter is visible, positioned away from the isolated uterine artery, highlighting the separation achieved during dissection.
The surgeon performs a dissection on the opposite side to identify the uterine artery, mirroring the previous steps taken to isolate the artery from surrounding structures.
The surgeon visualizes the ureter, ensuring it is clearly identified and away from the uterine artery during the procedure.
The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the opposite side, mirroring the procedure performed on the initial side.
This significantly reduces the vascularity inside the fibroid and makes the surgery relatively bloodless.
At our institute we prefer not to use vasopressin for any patient undergoing fibroid surgery irrespective of the size of the fibroid or the number of fibroids.
We are a large volume center for performing fibroid surgery and we have found the technique
uterine artery ligation at its origin or coagulation to be extremely effective in reducing the blood loss in patients undergoing laparoscopic myomectomy.
Following the uterine incision, the surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid, providing traction and counter-traction to facilitate the enucleation of the fibroid from its bed.
The video shows a deep-lying intramural fibroid within the uterine wall, visible after the uterine incision and traction with the myoma screw.
The fibroid is completely separated from the myoma bed, revealing the underlying endometrium.
The endometrial cavity is visibly intact and has not been opened during the procedure.
However, even if you were to open the endometrium cavity, it usually does not make any difference.
The surgical field remains bloodless as the surgeon continues the procedure, despite the use of vasopressin.
The surgeon closes the myoma bed using multilayered suturing with a barbed suture, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
The advantage with the barbed suture is that you do not need to take knots which saves surgical time and has adequate distribution of tissue and strength across the line of the suture.

The surgeon applies continuous sutures to the deeper layer of the myoma bed. Once the base of the cavity is obliterated, the superficial layer is closed using ipsilateral or contralateral baseball suturing technique.
The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the barbed suture to minimize its exposure to the peritoneal cavity, aiming to reduce the risk of omental or bowel adhesions.
The surgeon applies barbed sutures in a baseball manner to close the superficial layer of the myoma bed, maintaining a bloodless surgical field.
The difficulty with vasopressin is that one the anesthetist is not very comfortable and second you need to finish off the suturing pretty fast.
The surgeon completes the myomectomy by suturing the uterine opening, achieving closure with minimal blood loss visible in the surgical field.
